title: About
text:
I’m Vaibhav Nakrani, a Machine Learning and Agentic AI engineer. What I do, in one line: read modern research, pull out the idea that matters, and implement it against the hardest version of the problem in front of me.
That last part is the whole game. A lot of ML lives in the gap between a paper that works on a benchmark and a system that holds up at 4K @ 30 FPS in front of real users. I’m comfortable on both sides of that gap, and I think the people who can move between them, keeping the science honest while making the engineering ship, are the ones who actually solve the tough problems.
A few of those problems I’ve taken on:
The throughline is a balance between theory and engineering: I’ll dig into why a model behaves the way it does (interpretability with Captum, attention maps, occlusion studies, rigorous data cleaning) and then own the unglamorous path to production on AWS/GCP. My core stack is PyTorch, the YOLO/SAM/DETR family for vision, and LangGraph with the OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs for agents.
A recent review put it better than I can:

title: About
text:
Why this blog
Most ML explanations either hand-wave the intuition or drown you in notation. I write the version I wish I’d had, building ideas up one step at a time until they click, with the math kept honest but never used as a smokescreen. Expect posts where I take a piece of modern research and rebuild it from the ground up: diffusion, agents, computer vision, and whatever I’m currently figuring out.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
text:
Suppose a model tensor is stored in NVFP4. It is tempting to picture every entry as a miniature, self-contained FP16 value: four bits go in, one approximate real number comes out, and the rest is a hardware detail.
That picture is incomplete. The four bits only select from a tiny set of normalized values. To reconstruct a value in the tensor’s actual range, NVFP4 also needs a scale shared by a small block and another scale shared by the whole tensor.
The question I want to resolve is therefore narrower than “Is FP4 good?” It is: what job do the payload, block scale, and tensor scale each perform, and when can that representation help a real workload?
I will assume familiarity with tensors, ordinary floating point, quantization, and matrix multiplication. Everything specific to FP4 and NVIDIA’s implementation starts from scratch.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: Four bits do not provide many choices
text:
Four bits do not provide many choices
A floating-point layout is often named by how many exponent and mantissa bits it contains. E2M1 has one sign bit, two exponent bits, and one mantissa bit. Together, they make a four-bit payload, so E2M1 is one kind of FP4.
Ignoring the sign, its finite magnitudes are:
0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6
The sign bit mirrors the nonzero values below zero. This is a very small codebook, meaning the set of values the bit patterns can select. NVIDIA documents this E2M1 payload as the element format used by NVFP4 (Transformer Engine documentation).
Now consider a block whose useful values cluster near 0.01, and another whose values cluster near 100. A bare E2M1 codebook cannot closely cover both neighborhoods. Its largest finite magnitude is 6, and the gaps between available values are large relative to many tensors.
Quantization solves this by treating the codebook values as numbers in a local unit rather than final tensor values. A scale defines that unit. The payload says “four units”; the scale says how large one unit is here.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: The smallest example: the same payload, two real values
text:
The smallest example: the same payload, two real values
NVFP4 reconstructs a value with three factors:
x = x_e2m1 × s_block × s_global
Their scopes are more important than their names:

x_e2m1 is the four-bit code stored for one element.
s_block is a scale shared by a local block of 16 elements.
s_global is a scale shared by the whole tensor.

Take two 16-value blocks and fix the tensor-wide scale at s_global = 0.25.
Tensor-wide scale: s_global = 0.25

Block A: s_block = 1.0       Block B: s_block = 1.5
         payload = 4                  payload = 4
              |                            |
              v                            v
      4 × 1.0 × 0.25 = 1.0        4 × 1.5 × 0.25 = 1.5
Both elements contain the same E2M1 payload. Yet one reconstructs to 1.0 and the other to 1.5, because each block uses a different local unit. The payload is not a standalone measurement; it is a code interpreted within two shared coordinate systems.
The explorer below lets you vary those three factors separately. Choose an exact E2M1 payload, then move the block scale or tensor scale. The block control changes the unit for one toy 16-value neighborhood; the tensor control changes the unit shared by every block. The blue result is the reconstructed value, not a quantization-error or model-quality measurement.

  Toy explorer: one stored code, two scaling levels
  Toy calculation, not benchmark data. The controls use exact codebook values and simplified scale choices to expose the reconstruction rule.
  
    
      Stored E2M1 value
      
        −6−4−3
        −2−1.5−1
        −0.500.5
        11.52
        346
      
      Selects one normalized codebook value.
    
    
      Block scale: 1.50
      
      Shared by this toy block of 16 values.
    
    
      Tensor scale: 0.25
      
      Shared by every block in the toy tensor.
    
  
  
  4 × 1.50 × 0.25 = 1.50
  
    PayloadChooses a normalized E2M1 value for one element.
    Block scaleChanges the local unit for one 16-value neighborhood.
    Tensor scalePlaces all local units within one tensor-wide range.
  
  With the defaults, the stored code 4 becomes 1.5 in real tensor units.


Try leaving the payload at 4 and changing only the block scale from 1.0 to 1.5. The selected four-bit value does not move, but the reconstructed result does. Changing the tensor scale then moves every local unit together. This is the division of labor that the phrase “two-level scaling” can otherwise hide.
Why two levels instead of one? The block scale adapts the codebook to a small neighborhood, where values are more likely to have a similar range. The global scale places those local scales within the range that their own storage format can represent. Transformer Engine describes the same hierarchy and derives the global scale from the tensor’s maximum absolute value before deriving local scales from block maxima (Transformer Engine documentation).
The format names can now be attached without hiding their roles:

The element payload is E2M1, a four-bit floating-point code.
Each group of 16 payloads shares an E4M3 scale. E4M3 is an eight-bit floating-point format with four exponent and three mantissa bits, so its scale can take fractional values between powers of two.
The tensor shares an FP32 global scale, stored in ordinary 32-bit floating point.

This hierarchy is what two-level scaling means in NVFP4: a local scale per block, followed by a global scale per tensor. Per-block scaling adapts to variation among small groups. Per-tensor scaling provides one range-setting factor for the entire tensor. Neither phrase says that every operation or every piece of model state is stored at that precision.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: “4-bit” is a payload count, not a memory measurement
text:
“4-bit” is a payload count, not a memory measurement
Once the local scale is visible, a common storage shortcut breaks.
One block contains 16 four-bit payloads and one eight-bit local scale:
16 × 4 + 8 = 72 bits
Amortized across the block:
72 / 16 = 4.5 bits per value
So the basic payload-plus-local-scale representation is already 4.5 bits per value, not four. The tensor’s FP32 global scale adds a small amortized cost whose size depends on the number of elements in that tensor.
The next explorer makes that amortization visible. Move the slider to change the number of 16-value blocks in one toy tensor. It counts four payload bits per value, one eight-bit local scale per block, and one 32-bit global scale for the tensor. It intentionally excludes padding, alignment, duplicate layouts, high-precision components, and runtime buffers, so its result is a representation calculation rather than an application-memory forecast.

  Toy explorer: payload bits plus scale metadata
  Defined assumptions: 16 values per block, 4 payload bits per value, one 8-bit block scale, and one 32-bit tensor scale. No padding or runtime state is included.
  
    Tensor size: 1,024 values
    
    Each step doubles the number of 16-value blocks.
  
  
    
    
    
  
  
    E2M1 payloads
    E4M3 block scales
    FP32 tensor scale
  
  
    4,096 bitspayload
    544 bitsall scale metadata
    4.531 bits/valueunder these assumptions
  
  At 1,024 values, the single global scale is already thinly amortized. The local scale still contributes 0.5 bit per value.


At the smallest setting, the one global scale is expensive because only 16 values share it. As the toy tensor grows, that one-time cost fades, while the local-scale cost remains fixed at 8 / 16 = 0.5 bit per value. The output approaches 4.5 bits per value but never turns the calculation into a full-memory estimate.
Even 4.5 bits is not a full runtime-memory prediction. Transformer Engine documents padded and swizzled scale layouts and a TN-only NVFP4 GEMM layout; training may also keep rowwise and columnwise representations of weights (Transformer Engine documentation). Alignment, padding, higher-precision layers, activations, optimizer state, KV cache, workspaces, and framework allocations all remain outside the small calculation.
Comparing payloads alone, four bits are one quarter of a BF16 payload and one half of an FP8 payload. Those ratios are useful for understanding the upper-level opportunity. They do not establish a fourfold reduction in total application memory.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
text:
Generic FP4, MXFP4, and NVFP4
“FP4” is not a complete deployment specification. It can identify a four-bit element format such as E2M1 without telling us how a tensor is scaled. Two systems can store the same E2M1 payload and reconstruct it differently.
MXFP4 makes the comparison concrete. MXFP4, the FP4 member of the OCP microscaling family, also uses E2M1 payloads. It shares one E8M0 scale across 32 values. E8M0 has eight exponent bits and no mantissa, so its scale is restricted to powers of two. NVFP4 instead shares an E4M3 fractional scale across 16 values and adds the FP32 scale for the tensor (NVIDIA’s NVFP4 technical overview).









Property
Generic FP4 / E2M1
MXFP4
NVFP4




Element payload
May be E2M1
E2M1
E2M1


Local scale
Not specified by the name
E8M0, powers of two
E4M3, fractional FP8


Values per local scale
Not specified
32
16


Second scaling level
Not specified
None in the MX definition
One FP32 scale per tensor



The table is a comparison of representations, not model quality. NVFP4 gets two ways to fit a local distribution more closely: its scale covers half as many elements, and that scale can sit between powers of two. NVIDIA illustrates lower quantization error from these choices, but a representation-error example is not an end-to-end accuracy result (NVIDIA’s NVFP4 technical overview). Smaller blocks also mean more scale metadata and handling.
The better mental model is now precise: generic FP4 names a payload class; MXFP4 and NVFP4 specify different systems around an E2M1 payload.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: A format is not a training recipe
text:
A format is not a training recipe
If scaling were the whole story, low-precision training would amount to casting tensors and calling the same matrix multiplications. NVIDIA’s documented NVFP4 training path contains several additional mechanisms.
Two-dimensional weight scaling changes the grouping used for weights. Transformer Engine uses 16-by-16 tiles by default so rowwise and columnwise weight representations can support the forward and backward matrix multiplications. Activations and gradients use one-dimensional 16-value blocks.
Stochastic rounding chooses probabilistically between neighboring representable values instead of always taking the nearest one. For a gradient between two E2M1 values, the probabilities are chosen so the expected rounded value equals the original value. Transformer Engine enables this for gradient quantization. “Unbiased in expectation” does not mean an individual rounding is exact, and the added randomness has reproducibility implications.
A Random Hadamard Transform is an orthogonal mixing operation applied to selected operands before quantization. Its purpose here is to spread isolated large values across coordinates so that one outlier is less likely to set an unhelpful scale for an entire block. Transformer Engine currently documents this path for BF16 inputs and gradients used in selected weight-gradient operations (Transformer Engine documentation).
The broader recipe also retains higher precision for sensitive layers or operations. In other words, “NVFP4 training” is mixed-precision training built around NVFP4 matrix multiplications, not a claim that the whole training state and every computation use four bits.
NVIDIA reports pretraining a 12-billion-parameter model for 10 trillion tokens with loss and downstream accuracy comparable to an FP8 baseline using this combined method (NVFP4 training paper). This is evidence for the model, data, schedule, hardware, and recipe in that experiment. It is not evidence that naive E2M1 conversion works for arbitrary architectures, or that every model will match FP8.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: Hardware support still needs a full sentence
text:
Hardware support still needs a full sentence
NVFP4 is designed for native block-scaled matrix operations on NVIDIA Blackwell Tensor Cores. But “Blackwell supports NVFP4” leaves out which operation, device, software path, and tensor are involved.
Transformer Engine 2.16 documents NVFP4 training on compute capabilities SM100 and SM103, and inference on SM100 or later. It exposes the recipe to PyTorch and JAX through NVFP4BlockScaling, while also documenting layout and distributed-training constraints (Transformer Engine documentation). These are versioned software conditions, not permanent properties of every Blackwell deployment.
Inference has at least two separate quantization targets:

Weights and activations are operands used by model computations.
The KV cache stores attention keys and values from earlier tokens so autoregressive decoding does not recompute them.

TensorRT-LLM’s quantization matrix, inspected in the source package after its 2026-07-14 update, listed NVFP4 weights and activations on Blackwell SM100/103 and SM120. Its NVFP4 KV-cache support was narrower, limited to SM100/103, and the documented KV-cache combination required FP8 weights and activations (TensorRT-LLM quantization guide). Because this support changes with releases, the live matrix should be checked for the exact TensorRT-LLM version before deployment.
Training GEMMs, weight-and-activation inference, and KV-cache compression are therefore three different decisions. Each needs an exact compute capability, framework version, model and layout, calibration or training path, and quality test.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: Where speed and accuracy claims can fail
text:
Where speed and accuracy claims can fail
Native Tensor Core execution creates a credible path to higher matrix throughput and lower operand bandwidth. End-to-end performance improves only if supported matrix multiplications dominate the workload.
Quantization and scale handling consume work. Attention, communication, unsupported operators, memory bandwidth, small tensor shapes, and irregular layouts may dominate instead. A faster GEMM can simply move the bottleneck elsewhere. Peak hardware throughput is not a substitute for measuring prefill latency, decode latency, generation throughput, or training step time at the intended shapes and batch sizes.
Accuracy needs a separate boundary. Finer local scaling can reduce the numerical error introduced while representing a tensor. Model quality also depends on where quantization is applied, how calibration is performed, whether training adapts to it, which computations stay at higher precision, and how errors propagate through the network. Lower error in a toy tensor does not prove equal language-model accuracy, much less equal behavior on rare or sensitive cases.
The strongest large-scale result cited above was produced by NVIDIA for a particular pretraining experiment. Independent behavior on a target model, task distribution, context length, and deployment stack remains unknown until it is measured.

title: NVFP4 Is a Scaling System, Not Just a 4-Bit Number
section: A decision test for an NVFP4 claim
text:
A decision test for an NVFP4 claim
When a benchmark or deployment proposal says “NVFP4,” I would unpack it in this order:

Name the quantized object. Is it weights, activations, training GEMM operands, or the KV cache?
Count the representation. Include local and global scales, padding, duplicate layouts, and any higher-precision components instead of counting only E2M1 payload bits.
Verify the supported path. Record the compute capability, software versions, model family, tensor layout, and allowed precision combination.
Describe how the artifact was made. Distinguish a supported pre-quantized checkpoint, calibrated post-training quantization, and training with the full mixed-precision recipe.
Validate quality on the intended workload. Compare with BF16 or FP8 on held-out tasks, long contexts, outlier-heavy inputs, and rare behaviors that matter.
Measure the system boundary. Report checkpoint size, peak memory, KV-cache size where relevant, end-to-end latency or step time, throughput, and quality under the same conditions.

If those questions are unanswered, “4-bit” tells us the width of the element payload and little more.
NVFP4’s useful idea is not merely squeezing a floating-point value into four bits. It is giving the same tiny E2M1 codebook different local units, then putting those units into a tensor-wide range. That hierarchy can represent tensors more closely than a coarser power-of-two scheme, and Blackwell hardware can execute supported operations natively. The remaining memory, accuracy, and speed claims belong to the whole mixed-precision system and the workload running through it, not to the four payload bits alone.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
text:
“Causal” and “bidirectional” sound like labels attached permanently to a video model. One model moves left to right; another sees the whole clip. Pick an architecture, inherit its behavior.
That picture is useful, but incomplete. A generator also needs a policy for deciding which parts of a noisy video may exchange information, and when. Once we look at that policy, causal and bidirectional generation become endpoints of a larger scheduling problem.
I want to build that idea with four chunks of an imaginary video. No benchmark is hiding in the diagrams. They show information availability, not speed or quality measurements.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: Start with what each position is allowed to know
text:
Start with what each position is allowed to know
Suppose we split a video into four consecutive chunks: A, B, C, and D. A chunk is simply a group of neighboring video positions. It might contain one frame, several frames, or latent video tokens rather than pixels.
In causal generation, a position may use an available prefix: the current chunk and earlier chunks, but not later chunks that have not been generated. When producing C, information from A and B can flow forward; D cannot flow backward.
In bidirectional generation, positions across the clip may condition on one another while the clip is being refined. A rough state in A can influence D, and D can influence A. “Bidirectional” describes information flow, not playback direction. The finished video still plays from beginning to end.
The tempting model is therefore:

causal means A → B → C → D;
bidirectional means {A, B, C, D} are refined together.

Nothing is wrong with those endpoints. The mistake is assuming there can be no useful schedule between them.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: There are two kinds of time on the page
text:
There are two kinds of time on the page
Diffusion adds a second clock. Video time tells us whether a chunk appears early or late in the clip. Denoising time tells us how far a noisy representation has been refined toward a clean one.
The grid below keeps these clocks separate. Columns run through the video. Rows move from a rough, noisy state toward a ready state. A row is not another moment in the story; it is another stage in the computation.





Aearliest


B


C


Dlatest


rough state


A · rough


B · rough


C · rough


D · rough


more refined


A · refining


B · refining


C · refining


D · refining


ready state


A · ready


B · ready


C · ready


D · ready



rough partly refined ready

This separation matters because a schedule can change along either axis. It can group more or fewer neighboring video positions, and it can change those groups as denoising progresses.

Pause and predict: must D be completely unknown until A is ready? A strictly causal schedule says yes. A hybrid schedule does not have to.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
text:
The rigid endpoints
The tabs show two idealized reveal orders. Step through them from left to right. “Available” means the scheduler allows that region to participate; it does not mean the model has rendered a viewable frame at that instant.

BidirectionalCausal · after ACausal · after B







A


B


C


D


1 · rough


available


available


available


available


2 · refine


together


together


together


together


3 · ready


ready


ready


ready


ready


All four chunks enter the computation together and can help establish a global plan. The price of this simple picture is that the full working set must be handled together; it also does not naturally emit a completed prefix while later chunks remain absent.







A


B

D


rough

A is completed without future chunks. This makes a finished prefix possible, but A cannot use evidence that will appear only in C or D.







A

B may use A as context. C and D still cannot help. Repeating the pattern eventually completes the clip from left to right.



These diagrams deliberately exaggerate the contrast. Real systems differ in caching, attention masks, chunk size, and what counts as “ready.” The grid gives us a vocabulary for asking about those details instead of treating the labels as complete specifications.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: A schedule can live between the endpoints
text:
A schedule can live between the endpoints
Flex-Forcing makes chunking flexible over both the temporal dimension and denoising steps. The same video diffusion model can operate under bidirectional, autoregressive, and hybrid generation regimes. Its project page describes larger chunks as closer to global bidirectional planning and smaller chunks as closer to autoregressive streaming.
Here is a conceptual four-chunk hybrid. It is an explanatory sketch, not the paper’s exact tensor layout or a reproduced experimental configuration.

1 · rough plan2 · finish A3 · finish B4 · complete







A


B


C


D


rough


global hint


global hint


global hint


global hint


refine


pending


pending


pending


pending


ready


pending


pending


pending


pending


All chunks receive a rough global context. Later positions are not finished, but they are not entirely absent either. This is the bidirectional part of the mental model.







A


B


C


D


rough


global hint


global hint


global hint


global hint


refine


refined


planned


planned


planned


ready


A ready


pending


pending


pending


A can now be completed while the rough plan for B–D remains available. Fine synthesis moves forward even though coarse planning did not.







A


B


C


D


rough


global hint


global hint


global hint


global hint


refine


past context


refined


planned


planned


ready


A ready


B ready


pending


pending


B uses the completed prefix and the wider plan. Call this “autoregressive synthesis within the plan”: left-to-right completion—not that every internal operation must be strictly causal.







A


B


C


D


rough


global hint


global hint


global hint


global hint


refine


refined


refined


refined


refined


ready


A ready


B ready


C ready


D ready


The result is still an ordinary video. What changed was the reveal order of intermediate information.



The paper’s claim is broader than this toy schedule. Flex-Forcing defines flexible chunking jointly over video positions and denoising steps, supports chunk sizes chosen for different device budgets, and permits any-order, any-timestep autoregressive generation without a strict causal constraint. The authors also report better video quality, long-video stability, and faster inference than rigid-schedule baselines across their experiments (paper abstract; project results). Those are results for the evaluated configurations, not a guarantee that every hybrid schedule improves every metric.
The project page offers one concrete example of why the schedule itself matters. For a five-second video represented by 21 latent frames, it reports that a coarse-to-fine chunk configuration [9, 6, 6] lies on its measured quality–speed frontier and outperforms uniform [7, 7, 7] and its Self-Forcing baseline on the displayed measures. The numbers describe chunk configurations in that experiment; they are not denoising-step counts or a universal recipe.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: The policy changes the questions we should ask
text:
The policy changes the questions we should ask
Once generation becomes a schedule, “Is this model causal?” is too coarse. More useful questions are:

Which video positions are grouped into a chunk?
Can rough future positions influence the current chunk?
Does chunk size change along the denoising trajectory?
When is the first usable prefix available?
Which intermediate states must remain in memory?
Can a later chunk be edited without rebuilding the whole sequence?

Different answers can trade global planning, streaming behavior, memory use, and fine-grained control. The direction of those trade-offs is plausible from the information-flow pattern, but their size requires matched experiments on the same model, outputs, hardware, and quality target. A grid cannot produce a latency number.
Flex-Forcing also demonstrates why “inference policy” is a useful phrase rather than just a metaphor. Its project page describes editing at low-level refinement steps while keeping high-level planning fixed, and allowing a chunk to attend to both past and future tokens. The schedule determines which information can move and which parts can change.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: LingBot exposes a different kind of inference policy
text:
LingBot exposes a different kind of inference policy
LingBot-Video is useful here as a contrast, not as another Flex-Forcing implementation. Its public repository and model card expose a user-facing workflow with prompt rewriting, base video generation, an optional refiner, and multiple serving paths. The documented MoE text-to-video command uses a 40-step base stage followed by an 8-step refiner stage.
That sequence is also an inference policy: it decides how a prompt is prepared and how generation is divided across two denoising stages. But it operates along different axes from Flex-Forcing’s joint temporal-and-denoising chunk schedule. 40 + 8 does not tell us whether information flows causally or bidirectionally across video chunks, and the public LingBot materials cited here do not claim Flex-Forcing’s mechanism.
The comparison therefore stops at a shared lesson: a model’s weights and architecture do not completely describe the behavior a user receives. Scheduling, refinement, conditioning, and serving choices are part of the generation path. The two systems expose different choices, so their counters cannot be placed on one speed scale without a matched evaluation.

title: A Visual Guide to Causal, Bidirectional, and Hybrid Inference in Video Generation
section: A compact way to explain it back
text:
A compact way to explain it back
Try drawing a grid with video position on the horizontal axis and denoising progress on the vertical axis.
A rigid bidirectional schedule refines a large region together. A rigid causal schedule completes regions from left to right, leaving future regions unavailable. A flexible schedule can make rough information available across a wide region, then complete finer regions in an autoregressive order. Flex-Forcing turns that two-axis chunking pattern into a training and inference framework for one model.
This mental model establishes how causal and bidirectional behavior can become scheduling choices. It does not establish the best schedule for a deployment, the memory required by an implementation, or a speed comparison with LingBot. Those answers need released implementations or matched measurements.
The next time a video model is called “causal” or “bidirectional,” the useful follow-up is not “which one is better?” It is: what information becomes available at each point in video time and denoising time?

title: Diffusion Without the Hand-Waving
text:
The cleanest way to understand diffusion is to build it up one step at a time. Here’s the version that finally made it click.
Start with the goal. You want to turn random noise into a realistic image.
Now imagine you had a magic function. You show it any image and it tells you how realistic that image is. A clear photo scores high. Pure static scores low.
The useful part isn’t the score itself. It’s the gradient of that score with respect to the pixels. That gradient tells you exactly which pixels to change, and in which direction, to make the image look a little more real. Follow it over and over and noise slowly turns into a real image.
The problem is nobody can hand you that function.
The trick that makes the whole field work. You can learn that gradient without ever building the function. Take a real image. Add a known amount of noise to it. Train a network to predict the noise you added. That predicted noise is, give or take a scaling factor, the gradient you wanted. Subtract a bit of it and the image moves toward “more realistic.”
The network does not learn to clean up images. It learns which direction points toward real data. Denoising is just what that looks like from the outside. The proper name for that direction is the score, but you can keep thinking of it as a gradient.
Training is boring on purpose. Take images, add a random amount of noise to each, ask the model to predict the noise, penalize it with mean squared error. Repeat millions of times. You also feed in how much noise was added, so the model knows how messy the picture currently is. ( the magical timestep )
Generation just runs it backward. Start from pure noise. Ask the model which direction is more realistic. Take a small step that way. Ask again. Step again. Repeat until an image appears. The steps are small because the model’s guess is only approximate, especially early on when the input looks nothing like real data.
That stepping strategy is the sampler. DDPM, DDIM, Euler, DPM-Solver. They teach the model nothing new. They’re just different ways of walking downhill. The right mental model is optimizers. SGD versus Adam. Same loss surface, different path, different speed. A good sampler gets you there in 20 steps instead of 1000.
So far this was on raw pixels. In practice you don’t work on pixels, and you also want to control what gets generated. Two extra pieces handle that, and both are trained ahead of time and then frozen.
The VAE is just a compressor. It’s trained separately, before any diffusion, with one job: squash an image into a small latent and decode it back faithfully. It never sees noise and knows nothing about diffusion. Once trained, you freeze it. All the diffusion now happens on these small latents instead of big images, which is what makes it cheap.



vae


The text encoder is the text half of a CLIP model, also trained earlier and frozen. Its job is to turn a caption into an embedding. That embedding is how the prompt steers generation. It enters the U-Net through cross-attention, so the gradient now points toward realistic and matches this caption.
Here’s the actual training flow, start to finish: - Take an (image, caption) pair. - Caption goes through the frozen text encoder. Out comes a text embedding. - Image goes through the frozen VAE encoder. Out comes a clean latent. - Pick a random timestep. Blend the clean latent with random noise according to that timestep. Low timestep is mostly clean latent, high timestep is mostly noise. This is a one-shot formula, you do not iterate here. - That noisy latent, the timestep, and the text embedding go into the U-Net. - The U-Net predicts the noise that was added. - Loss is MSE between the real noise and the predicted noise. Only the U-Net updates. VAE and text encoder stay frozen.



diffusion training process


Step 4 is the thing people get stuck on. You do not add noise to the image. You encode the image to a latent first, then add noise to the latent with a formula. The clean latent is the target, the noise is what the model predicts.
Generation is the same pipeline in reverse:

Caption through the text encoder, get the embedding.
Start from a pure noise latent.
Each step, the U-Net predicts the noise from the current latent, the timestep, and the text embedding. The sampler uses that to step to a slightly cleaner latent. Repeat.
You end with a clean latent. Run it through the VAE decoder to get the final image.




forward pass diffusion


And the network itself is swappable. Replace the U-Net with something else and you get modern DiT backbones. Same training flow, same everything else.
So the whole thing is still three pieces: A direction that says which way is more realistic. A network that learns it by predicting noise. A sampler that walks that direction from noise to image.
The VAE and text encoder are just the frozen helpers that make it cheap and controllable. Everything else is detail.
Optional: both the vae and the text encoder can be fine-tuned when tuning these models. text encoder tuning is more beneficial generally than vae from my expreiments.
Inspired by fast.ai Practical Deep Learning, Lesson 9. If you want the long version, the lesson has the full lecture video walking through all of this end to end. Grab a coffee, it’s worth the couple of hours.
